entries.label: Hybrid Agent Pipeline
entries.workingDef: An architectural pattern for multi-agent systems in which some agents are implemented as LLM-based reasoning components while others are purely deterministic (e.g., database lookups, rule-based logic), with the two types interleaved in a single pipeline. Proponents argue this yields better reliability, lower latency, and reduced hallucination risk by restricting LLM use to tasks that require natural-language reasoning and delegating structured lookups to code.

entries.label: Privacy by Design
entries.workingDef: An architectural principle applied in AI deployment contexts where sensitive data — such as proprietary CAD geometry — must not leave a controlled environment. In this framing, on-premise model hosting (e.g., running open-weight models on local GPU hardware) is treated not as a performance optimisation but as a compliance and confidentiality requirement. Distinct from the broader regulatory usage of the term in GDPR contexts, though related.

entries.label: Emergent Modularity
entries.workingDef: A training objective and model property in which a Mixture-of-Experts architecture develops coherent, domain-aligned expert groupings without relying on predefined human labels. Achieved in EMO (Allen AI, 2026) by constraining all tokens within a document to route through a shared expert pool, so recurring expert groups self-organise from data structure rather than externally imposed categories. Enables selective deployment of small expert subsets for specific tasks while retaining near full-model performance.

sources.title: EMO: Pretraining mixture of experts for emergent modularity

entries.label: Document-Boundary Routing
entries.workingDef: A training technique introduced in EMO (Allen AI, 2026) in which all tokens within a single document are constrained to activate experts drawn from a shared, document-level expert pool. The pool is selected by averaging per-token router preferences across the document. This uses document boundaries as a weak, unlabelled supervisory signal to encourage higher-level domain specialisation in MoE experts, avoiding the need for explicit domain labels on the pretraining corpus.

sources.title: EMO: Pretraining mixture of experts for emergent modularity

entries.label: Selective Expert Use
entries.workingDef: An inference-time capability of certain Mixture-of-Experts models (notably EMO, Allen AI 2026) whereby only a small, task-relevant subset of total experts is loaded and activated, while the remaining experts are discarded. Subset selection is guided by routing-usage statistics on a small validation sample. Enables improved memory and compute efficiency for specific tasks without separate fine-tuned models. Contrasts with standard MoE behaviour where full expert access is required to avoid significant performance degradation.

entries.label: Expert Subset Specialization
entries.workingDef: The observed phenomenon in MoE models where identifiable groups of experts cluster around higher-level semantic domains (e.g., mathematics, code, biomedical text) rather than low-level lexical patterns such as punctuation or prepositions. Standard MoE training tends to produce the latter; EMO's document-boundary routing is designed to produce the former. Linked to the broader goal of composable and interpretable model architectures.

entries.label: AI-Generated Quotation Error
entries.workingDef: A class of AI-assisted journalism failure in which text generated by an AI tool — typically a summary or paraphrase — is mistakenly treated and published as a verbatim quotation from a real person. The NYT Editors' Note of May 2026 is a documented primary-source example: a reporter published an AI-rendered summary of Pierre Poilievre's views as a direct quote. Distinct from general hallucination in that the error involves misattribution of words to a named individual, with direct reputational and factual stakes.

The term is used by operators, planners, and critics alike, though 'hyperscale' has no universal threshold; usage ranges from technical capacity descriptors to policy shorthand for facilities whose energy demands rival those of entire states.

entries.label: Benchmaxxing
entries.workingDef: The practice of optimizing a model specifically to improve its score on a known benchmark without achieving corresponding gains in real-world performance. Coined as a portmanteau of 'benchmark' and 'maxxing,' the term describes deliberate or incidental overfitting to public test sets — including training on data that closely resembles evaluation splits. Closely related to benchmark gaming and evaluation gaming, but implies an active, targeted optimization strategy rather than accidental saturation.

entries.label: Test-Set Contamination
entries.workingDef: The condition in which data drawn from or closely resembling a held-out evaluation set appears in a model's training data, inflating benchmark scores relative to true generalisation performance. In ASR and LLM contexts alike, contamination may be explicit (direct inclusion of test examples) or distributional (training on data from the same source or domain as the test set). Motivates the use of private or undisclosed evaluation sets.

entries.label: Wafer-Scale Engine
entries.workingDef: A chip architecture pioneered by Cerebras Systems in which the entire silicon wafer — rather than individual dies cut from it — is treated as a single integrated chip. By laying wiring across the scribe lines that normally separate reticle exposures, Cerebras eliminates chip-to-chip interconnects within the device, yielding extremely high on-chip SRAM and memory bandwidth. The WSE-3, for example, offers ~44GB of SRAM at ~21 PB/s bandwidth — roughly 6,000× the memory bandwidth of an H100 GPU — making it highly suited to memory-bandwidth-bound inference workloads, though its limited off-chip scaling constrains large-model training use cases.

entries.label: Prefill–Decode Split
entries.workingDef: A decomposition of LLM inference into two functionally distinct phases with different hardware requirements: (1) prefill, which encodes the full input context into an internal state and is highly parallelisable and compute-bound; and (2) decode, which autoregressively generates output tokens by reading both the KV cache (context) and model weights on every step, and is serial and memory-bandwidth-bound. The split has practical implications for chip selection — prefill favours high-compute GPUs while decode favours high-bandwidth, low-latency memory — and motivates heterogeneous or disaggregated inference architectures.

sources.title: The Inference Shift

entries.label: Heterogeneous AI Compute
entries.workingDef: A framing for the emerging AI infrastructure landscape in which no single chip architecture dominates all workloads. Contrasted with the 'GPU era' where Nvidia's GPUs handled both training and inference, heterogeneous compute argues that future AI deployments will mix purpose-built silicon — e.g., wafer-scale chips for low-latency inference, specialised training accelerators, edge chips — matched to specific workload profiles. The article treats Cerebras' IPO as a signal of this structural shift.

sources.title: The Inference Shift

entries.label: Inference Shift
entries.workingDef: A periodization framing — introduced and elaborated by Ben Thompson in this Stratechery piece — for the structural transition in AI compute demand from training-dominated GPU workloads to inference-dominated, increasingly heterogeneous hardware deployments. The framing argues that as agent usage scales, inference becomes the primary driver of compute spend, creating market conditions for specialised inference silicon distinct from Nvidia's GPU-centric stack. As a named framing by a single analyst, its adoption and durability as industry terminology remains to be seen.

entries.label: Zombie Internet
entries.workingDef: A term coined by Jason Koebler to describe the current state of the internet as neither purely bot-driven (the 'Dead Internet' scenario) nor authentically human — but a hybrid ecology in which humans using AI interact with other humans using AI, humans interact with bots, and automated accounts interact with real people. The framing captures the cognitive and epistemic exhaustion of navigating spaces where the provenance of any given piece of content is genuinely unclear. Distinguished from 'Dead Internet Theory' in that the internet is not dead but animated by something ambiguous and harder to filter: zombie-like human-AI hybrids rather than pure bot-spam.

entries.label: Dead Internet Theory
entries.workingDef: A pre-existing internet culture theory holding that large portions of internet traffic and content are generated entirely by bots interacting with other bots, with human presence diminishing to a marginal role. Invoked here as the foil to the 'Zombie Internet' framing — the Dead Internet imagines a clean separation (bots vs. humans) whereas the Zombie Internet describes a messier, harder-to-detect hybrid. The term precedes the current AI content wave but has been revived as context for understanding AI's effect on online authenticity.

entries.label: AI Slop

Distinct from hallucination (which is a model failure) and from AI-assisted writing (which may be high quality); slop specifically connotes volume-over-quality content farms. The term is gaining traction in critical discourse about AI's effect on information environments.

entries.label: AI-Generated Exploit
entries.workingDef: An exploit or malicious payload whose code shows evidence of having been drafted or refined using a large language model — for example, hallucinated CVSS scores, structured 'textbook' formatting consistent with LLM training data, or AI-optimised payload tuning. Google Threat Intelligence Group's May 2026 report is the first public instance of a major threat-intelligence organisation attributing a zero-day exploit to AI-assisted development. The category is distinct from AI-discovered vulnerabilities (where AI finds flaws in legitimate code); here AI is used by the attacker to construct the offensive payload. Forensic identification relies partly on LLM output artefacts embedded in the code.

entries.label: Persona-Driven Jailbreaking
entries.workingDef: A jailbreaking technique in which an attacker instructs an AI model to adopt a persona — such as a security expert — in order to elicit responses the model would otherwise refuse, such as vulnerability analysis or exploit generation. Documented by Google GTIG as an observed attacker pattern in 2026. Related to but distinct from generic prompt injection; persona-driven jailbreaking relies on role-framing rather than instruction overrides or data-plane attacks.

entries.label: Hallucinated CVSS Score
entries.workingDef: A Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) score embedded in attacker-authored exploit code that is factually incorrect or fabricated — identified by Google GTIG as a forensic signal indicating the code was produced with LLM assistance. The hallucinated score is a characteristic LLM output artefact: the model generates plausible-looking metadata without verifying its accuracy. Its presence in a malicious script provides defenders with a new category of attribution evidence for AI-assisted attacks.

The term borrows from the Palantir playbook of placing technical staff inside enterprises rather than selling software at arm's length. Distinct from conventional sales engineering; the role involves hands-on rearchitecting of business processes around AI capabilities rather than pre-sales demos or post-sales support.

entries.label: Enterprise Philosophy
entries.workingDef: Ben Thompson's framing (Stratechery, 2024–2026) for the third wave of technology business logic, distinct from the consumer and productivity-copilot philosophies. Under this framing, the buyer — not the end user — drives adoption; the motivation is bottom-line improvement through cost reduction and revenue growth; and AI systems act as worker replacements rather than worker augmentations. Thompson argues the correct historical analogy is the 1970s mainframe wave (accounting/ERP software eliminating back-office roles), not SaaS or the consumer internet.

entries.label: Agent Count Fallacy
entries.workingDef: The rhetorical or analytical error of treating the number of AI agents deployed or running as a meaningful metric of capability, productivity, or complexity. Boris Mann's 2026 formulation — 'I have 11 AI agents' is as meaningful as 'I have 11 spreadsheets' — frames agent count as an abstraction so coarse it tells you almost nothing about what work is actually being done. Related to agent-washing: agent count can function as a vanity metric that inflates perceived AI adoption.

entries.label: Incognito Chat
entries.workingDef: A chat mode in Meta AI (and WhatsApp) that combines no server-side conversation logging with end-to-end encryption, so that messages are not retained after a session ends and cannot be read by Meta. Meta positions it as distinct from other AI incognito or temporary-chat modes, which may still transmit cleartext to the provider's servers even if logs are deleted on a schedule.

entries.label: AGI
entries.workingDef: A term contested across multiple communities. OpenAI/industry capability framings — prominently foregrounded in this article's trial evidence — use AGI to mean systems that broadly equal or surpass human knowledge and ability across economically valuable tasks, a threshold that served as the explicit founding motivation for OpenAI. Theoretical/academic lineages (Hutter, Goertzel) define it as generality across cognitive tasks at human level, a more abstract construct. Skeptics (Marcus, Bender, Mitchell) argue the term has no stable operational definition: each speaker defines it to fit their narrative and the goalposts shift with marketing cycles. The Musk v. Altman trial made the contestation concrete — both sides invoked AGI as the supreme motivating stake while disagreeing entirely about who should control it.

The Musk v. Altman trial gave the concept renewed force: testimony from multiple senior insiders alleged a pattern of deception and power-seeking at the highest levels of the industry's leading lab.

entries.label: AI as Normal Technology
entries.workingDef: A framework advanced by Sayash Kapoor and Arvind Narayanan (AI Snake Oil) arguing that AI's economic and social impacts follow the diffusion patterns of prior general-purpose technologies — subject to speed limits such as organisational adoption, workflow change, and regulation — rather than constituting a categorically unprecedented disruption. Used to argue against extraordinary government intervention and rapid job-displacement claims. The framework is contested: critics (including Derek Thompson and others in the AI risk tradition) argue that AI's emergent capabilities and unknown-unknown risk profile make the 'normal technology' analogy insufficient for safety policy, even if it holds for labour market analysis.

Used to argue that extraordinary pre-deployment restrictions are less effective than building societal resilience, since attackers can exploit capabilities without going through slow organisational adoption. The balance shifts depending on domain (e.g., cyber vs. bio), capability type, and the speed of defensive tooling development.

Encompasses investments in cybersecurity infrastructure, bio-defence, regulatory capacity, and institutional readiness to respond to AI-enabled misuse. Contrasted with nonproliferation-style interventions, which the authors argue are brittle because they depend on physical or legal bottlenecks that AI erodes.

entries.label: ITBench-AA
entries.workingDef: A benchmark developed by Artificial Analysis and IBM, evaluating frontier AI models on agentic enterprise IT operations tasks — beginning with Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) and expanding to Financial Operations (FinOps) and CISO tasks. Tasks require agents to diagnose live Kubernetes incidents by reading logs, tracing dependencies, and identifying root-cause entities, scored via recall-gated precision. entries.label: Recall-Gated Precision
entries.workingDef: A scoring methodology used in agentic benchmarks (notably ITBench-AA) where a model receives zero credit unless it identifies all ground-truth root causes (full recall), after which its score equals the precision of its submitted answer set. This penalises over-investigation — submitting additional plausible but incorrect entities — and rewards minimal, accurate diagnosis over expansive exploration.

entries.label: Aggregator Theory
entries.workingDef: A framework coined by Ben Thompson (Stratechery) describing internet platforms — such as Google, Facebook, and Amazon — that intermediate between suppliers and users by controlling demand aggregation. Aggregators operate at near-zero marginal cost on the supply side, benefit from network effects on the user side, and extract value from suppliers who compete to reach the aggregator's audience. Distinct from traditional media or marketplace businesses in that the aggregator does not own or create supply. Used in this article to contrast Google's original high-margin advertising model with its emerging capital-intensive AI infrastructure posture.

entries.label: Productive Capital Business

Applied to Google Cloud and AI compute: the argument is that hyperscalers investing in data centres, TPUs, and AI infrastructure are transitioning from asset-light aggregators into productive-capital businesses whose value is driven by absolute return on deployed capital rather than marginal-cost economics.

entries.label: Direct Preference Optimization
entries.workingDef: A training method introduced by Rafailov et al. (2023) that optimizes a language model toward preferred outputs by directly maximizing the likelihood ratio of chosen over rejected completions, without requiring a separately trained reward model. Unlike RLHF, DPO incorporates the preference signal into the supervised loss. The term is most commonly encountered in chat alignment — tuning models to be more helpful, harmless, or accurate — but this article demonstrates it applies equally to objective, non-conversational structured generation tasks, where model-generated failure outputs serve as rejection pairs.

sources.title: Direct Preference Optimization Beyond Chatbots

entries.proposalReason: A new term with growing usage across the field; this article also usefully extends the concept beyond its canonical chat-alignment framing into objective structured tasks.

entries.label: Text Degeneration
entries.workingDef: A failure mode in autoregressive language models where the model enters a self-reinforcing repetition loop during inference, repeatedly generating the same token or phrase until the sequence reaches the maximum token limit. The article explains this as a distributional geometry problem: once a token dominates its own conditional distribution, each sampling step deepens the attractor, making exit without external intervention structurally improbable. Distinct from hallucination — the model is not fabricating content but stuck in a probability attractor — and not fully addressable by inference-layer interventions (repetition penalties, temperature) or by SFT alone, which has no training term that explicitly penalises completion-level repetition.

sources.title: Direct Preference Optimization Beyond Chatbots

entries.proposalReason: A well-defined, recurring failure mode in generative models that deserves a canonical entry; the article provides the clearest mechanistic explanation of it as a distributional geometry problem rather than a decoding artifact.

entries.label: Self-Generated Rejection Pairs
entries.workingDef: A methodology for constructing DPO training data without human preference labels, by using a model's own characteristic failure outputs as the 'rejected' examples in preference pairs. The approach exploits the observation that a model capable of performing a structured task also fails in predictable, characteristic ways — those failures are treated as negative training signals rather than discarded noise. Proposed by DharmaOCR as a way to apply DPO to objective, non-conversational tasks where human preference rankings are not available or meaningful.

entries.fullName: Self-Generated Rejection Pairs (DPO without Human Annotation)

entries.label: SFT Ceiling
entries.workingDef: The observed empirical limit on how much supervised fine-tuning alone can reduce certain structural failure modes — particularly text degeneration — in language models. The conjecture is that SFT's token-level loss objective has no term penalising completion-level failures; it optimises proximity to a task domain but cannot reshape the attractor geometry that produces repetition loops. Distinct from capability ceiling: SFT and degeneration resistance can move independently, meaning a model can gain task fluency while its degeneration rate worsens. First clearly articulated in the DharmaOCR benchmark analysis.

The concept is closely tied to his notion of 'token capital' as a new factor of production alongside human capital.

entries.label: Token Capital
entries.workingDef: A term introduced by Satya Nadella in this interview to describe AI inference and model-use capacity as a new factor of production alongside human capital. In this framing, firms in the AI era must manage and invest in 'token capital' — their accumulated capacity to run, fine-tune, and improve AI systems — as a distinct strategic asset. The concept extends resource-based views of the firm into the AI era but is so far a single-source, single-speaker coinage.

entries.fullName: Token Capital (AI Era Factor of Production)
entries.proposalReason: A potentially durable conceptual coinage from a CEO-level primary source; if adopted broadly, it could become a canonical framing term for AI business strategy.

entries.label: Clean Lineage
entries.workingDef: A term used in the Nadella interview to describe MAI models built from scratch without distillation from other frontier models or use of other models as teachers — establishing clear, unencumbered provenance for training data and methodology. The concept is linked to IP defensibility and the desire to operate independently of external model providers. Distinct from 'open-weight' concerns; about training-process independence rather than weight availability.

sources.title: An Interview with Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella About Finding Core Competencies

entries.proposalReason: Emerging term for a real and consequential distinction in how labs characterise model provenance and independence; worth watching as IP and model-lineage disputes intensify.

entries.label: Statement Retraction
entries.workingDef: The act of a company or spokesperson asking a journalist to replace a published statement with a revised version after publication, typically to soften or remove a commitment. In AI contexts, this pattern has emerged when organisations walk back safety-adjacent language — such as commitments to human-in-the-loop oversight — under apparent strategic or commercial pressure. The practice raises questions about the gap between publicly stated AI governance principles and actual corporate intent.

sources.title: A quote from Emanuel Maiberg, 404 Media

entries.proposalReason: A new framing term for a notable editorial/governance pattern — companies quietly retracting safety-language statements post-publication.

entries.label: Project Stratos
entries.workingDef: A proposed hyperscale data center complex planned by Kevin O'Leary in Utah, originally spanning approximately 40,000 acres near the Locomotive Springs Waterfowl Management Area. Following community opposition and political pressure, O'Leary agreed to reduce the footprint to roughly 20,000 acres — still an area larger than Manhattan. The project has drawn scrutiny over water consumption, energy demand, and environmental impact, making it a high-profile instance of the land-use and resource tensions surrounding large-scale AI infrastructure buildout.

entries.label: EVA-Bench
entries.workingDef: A benchmark suite developed by ServiceNow AI for evaluating enterprise voice agents across realistic multi-domain scenarios. Version 2.0 covers 213 evaluation scenarios across 121 tools in three enterprise domains (airline customer service, IT service management, and healthcare HR service delivery), testing agent capabilities including authentication, multi-intent handling, adversarial caller behaviour, and unsatisfiable goal detection.

entries.label: SyGra
entries.workingDef: A graph-based synthetic data generation pipeline developed by ServiceNow AI for producing jointly consistent benchmark scenarios. SyGra generates user goals (as decision trees), initial scenario databases, and expected final database states together in a single pass to prevent silent inconsistencies between components — a design choice motivated by reproducibility requirements in agentic evaluation.

sources.title: EVA-Bench Data 2.0: 3 Domains, 121 Tools, 213 Scenarios

entries.proposalReason: New named system for synthetic benchmark data generation with a distinctive joint-generation architecture worth tracking.

entries.label: Unsatisfiable Goal Scenario
entries.workingDef: An evaluation pattern in which the user's stated objective cannot be fulfilled given the constraints of the scenario — included in benchmarks like EVA-Bench because production call volume is not all 'happy-path' and models reportedly perform worse on goals that cannot be resolved than on successful interactions. Distinct from adversarial scenarios (where the caller attempts to bypass rules) in that the failure is structural rather than intentional.

entries.label: Multi-Intent Call
entries.workingDef: A voice agent evaluation scenario type in which a single conversation contains multiple sequential user goals (up to four in EVA-Bench), testing an agent's ability to track state, switch contexts, and complete distinct tasks within one interaction. Contrasted with single-intent calls and adversarial calls as a distinct axis of difficulty in enterprise voice benchmarks.

entries.label: Code Provenance
entries.workingDef: The question of who is responsible for a piece of code and where it originated — gaining new salience in the AI era as AI-generated contributions break the traditional assumption that substantial code implies substantial human effort and good faith. Framed by open-source maintainers like Andreas Kling (Ladybird project) as distinct from the question of authorship: what matters is not whether code was typed by hand, but who accepts accountability for it entering a codebase.

sources.title: A quote from Andreas Kling

entries.proposalReason: The Ladybird project's decision to close public PRs names a novel framing: AI generation severs the effort-as-good-faith heuristic that open-source has always relied on. 'Code provenance' captures this gap between origin and accountability.

entries.label: Effort-as-Proxy Collapse
entries.workingDef: The breakdown of the longstanding heuristic in open-source development that a substantial code contribution implies substantial author effort, and that effort is a reasonable proxy for good faith. Named implicitly by Andreas Kling in the Ladybird project's policy change: AI code generation allows high-volume, low-effort submissions that are indistinguishable in size from carefully crafted work, invalidating the heuristic maintainers have relied on for triage and trust.

sources.title: A quote from Andreas Kling

entries.proposalReason: A precise failure-mode concept for the open-source contribution model under AI-generated code. Different from 'AI washing' — this is about the collapse of a specific epistemic signal maintainers use, not about mislabelling products.

entries.label: Maintainer Accountability
entries.workingDef: The principle, foregrounded by open-source projects responding to AI-generated contributions, that the person introducing a change to a codebase must be the person who vouches for it and answers for its consequences — independent of whether they wrote it. Andreas Kling's Ladybird policy articulates this as the operative standard replacing effort-based proxies: responsibility, not authorship, is what qualifies a contribution.

sources.title: A quote from Andreas Kling

entries.proposalReason: A distinct normative concept emerging from the AI-code-in-open-source debate — accountability decoupled from authorship. Likely to appear in more open-source governance discussions as AI contributions scale.

entries.label: Papal AI Encyclical
entries.workingDef: A formal doctrinal letter issued by a Pope addressing the ethical, spiritual, and societal dimensions of artificial intelligence. Pope Leo XIV's 'Magnifica humanitas: On safeguarding the human person in the time of artificial intelligence' (May 2026) is the first such encyclical, representing the Catholic Church's entry into AI governance discourse as an external moral authority. The encyclical frames AI as raising questions for theology, philosophy, and the humanities — not merely computer science — and calls for 'discernment' on AI's nature and societal impact.

sources.title: Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah's remarks on Pope Leo XIV's encyclical "Magnifica humanitas"

entries.proposalReason: A genuinely new category of governance artefact — a papal encyclical on AI — that warrants tracking as the Church formally enters the AI governance landscape.

entries.label: Global AI Equity
entries.workingDef: The framing — used by Olah in his Vatican remarks and echoed in broader policy discourse — that AI development concentrated in a handful of wealthy nations risks exacerbating global inequality if the gains of AI are not distributed across all populations, including the global poor. Proponents argue this is an unsolved structural problem requiring institutional mechanisms and moral pressure. Critics within the AI industry tend to frame AI access as a market outcome. The term overlaps with 'AI ethics' and 'responsible AI' debates but specifically foregrounds geopolitical and North/South distribution of AI benefit.

sources.title: Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah's remarks on Pope Leo XIV's encyclical "Magnifica humanitas"

entries.proposalReason: Olah names global AI equity as an unsolved structural problem requiring non-lab moral voices — a framing distinct from standard 'AI ethics' discourse and worth tracking as it emerges in governance conversations.

entries.label: AI Discernment
entries.workingDef: A term drawn from theological and philosophical tradition, used in 'Magnifica humanitas' and Olah's Vatican remarks to describe the deliberative process by which religious communities, civil society, and other non-technical actors evaluate the moral, spiritual, and social dimensions of AI systems. The framing positions discernment — patient, multi-stakeholder moral reasoning — as a necessary complement to technical AI development, and implicitly critiques purely technocratic governance. The term is used by Olah to invite external moral authority into AI governance, while remaining distinct from the technical 'alignment' framing used within AI safety research.

sources.title: Anthropic co-founder Chris Olah's remarks on Pope Leo XIV's encyclical "Magnifica humanitas"

entries.proposalReason: A newly prominent governance framing borrowed from theology, used by Olah to name the kind of moral reasoning he believes labs cannot do from the inside — a substantive conceptual contribution to AI governance vocabulary.
